A.notes: Builds percentage rollout with deterministic user bucketing and Redis caching upfront. Reasonable but exceeds 'simplest approach' — the percentage column and hashing logic aren't needed for a dark launch toggle.

B.notes: Boolean-only DB flag with Redis cache. Explicitly defers percentage rollouts: 'don't build that until you need it.' Good proportionality. ~30 lines claim is accurate. Loses a point for Redis caching layer when a simple DB read would suffice for one flag.

C.notes: Includes percentage rollout and user bucketing from the start. Uses in-memory ConcurrentHashMap (simpler than Redis cache), but builds gradual rollout infrastructure before it's needed. Good 'why not vendor' section.

D.notes: JSONB metadata column adds speculative flexibility. Suggests a frontend REST API endpoint — scope creep beyond what was asked. The allowed_account_ids in metadata is building targeting infrastructure prematurely.

E.notes: Redis-only approach is genuinely simple — no migration needed, instant toggling via CLI. Account-level targeting via Redis sets is elegant. Explicitly defers percentage rollouts. Loses a point because no persistence (Redis restart loses flags) which is a real operational concern left unaddressed.

F.notes: Best proportionality: starts with env var (truly simplest), graduates to DB flag, and only mentions vendor tools to explicitly dismiss them as overkill. Recommends option 2 with ~20 lines. The only system that actually offers the simplest-first approach the rubric rewards. Uses Python instead of Kotlin (stack mismatch) but the proportionality thinking is correct.

G.notes: Most over-engineered: builds percentage rollout AND allowed_ids array AND Redis caching AND deterministic hashing all upfront. The schema has 6 columns for a single dark-launch flag. Multi-step rollout plan with named test accounts suggests a feature management system, not the 'simplest approach.'

H.notes: Ratio as DOUBLE PRECISION plus JSONB metadata column is speculative design. Mentions pg_notify for sub-second propagation — additional infrastructure suggestion. Similar to A/C in complexity but the ratio-as-double adds unnecessary precision for a boolean use case.

analysis: System F is the clear winner on proportionality — it's the only response that starts with an environment variable (the actual simplest approach) and graduates to a DB flag, matching the rubric's emphasis on 'simplest adequate approach.' Systems B and E tie for second: B uses a boolean-only DB flag and explicitly defers percentage rollouts, while E takes a Redis-only approach that avoids migrations entirely. The middle tier (A, C, H) all build percentage-based gradual rollout infrastructure from the start — reasonable engineering but exceeding what 'simplest' demands for a single dark-launch flag. System D adds scope creep with a frontend API endpoint and JSONB metadata. System G is the most over-engineered, combining percentage rollouts, allowed_ids arrays, Redis caching, and deterministic hashing into what amounts to a mini feature-flag platform. The key differentiator across all responses is whether they build rollout infrastructure upfront or defer it — the rubric clearly rewards deferral, and most systems failed to resist the temptation to include gradual rollout from day one.
